message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  A new SARS-CoV-2 variant named Omicron (B.1.1.529) discovered initially in South Africa has recently been proposed as a variant of concern (VOC) by the World Health Organization, because of its high transmissibility and resistance to current vaccines and therapeutic antibodies. Therefore, rapid development of vaccines against prevalent variants including Omicron is urgently needed for COVID-19 prevention. Here, we designed a self-assembling ferritin-based nanoparticle (FNP) vaccine against the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant. The purified Fc-RBD
                  <jats:sub>Omicron</jats:sub>
                  automatically formed a dimer depending on the nature of the Fc tag, thus assembling onto the nanoparticles by the Fc-protein A tag interaction (FNP-Fc-RBD
                  <jats:sub>Omicron</jats:sub>
                  ). The results of hACE2-transgenic mice immunization showed that SARS-CoV-2 Omicron RBD-specific IgG titer induced by FNP-Fc-RBDOmicron was much higher than that by Fc-RBD
                  <jats:sub>Omicron</jats:sub>
                  . Consistently, the sera showed a higher neutralizing activity against SARS-CoV-2 Omicron BA.1 and BA.2 in the FNP-Fc-RBD
                  <jats:sub>Omicron</jats:sub>
                  immunized mice, indicating that immunization of a self-assembling ferritin-based nanoparticle vaccine offers a robust humoral immune response against Omicron variants. This study offers a great potential for the quick response of the emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants and affords versatility to develop universal vaccines against other emerging and reemerging coronaviruses in the future.
                </jats:p>
title: Development of a ferritin-based nanoparticle vaccine against the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant

reference.unstructured: Johns Hopkins Coronavirus Resource Center. accessed 2022 Jan 04. https://coronavirus.jhu.edu/
Liu, Y , Liu, J & Shi P. SARS-CoV-2 variants and vaccination.Zoonoses 2 (2022).
